questions.text: In the 1950s, two programs ran one after the other on the same machine. What stopped one program from reading the other's data?

options.A: Nothing — there was no protection between programs at all
options.B: The human operator removed each program from memory before the next ran
options.C: Programs were each given separate physical sections of the machine
options.D: Programs were not allowed to store data in memory at all

questions.explanation:
In the **1950s**, the OS was just a simple library of helper routines — there was no hardware memory protection. Programs ran one at a time, loaded and unloaded by a human operator.

**Why A and B are both acceptable:**
- **(A)** is technically correct: at the hardware level, there was *no* protection at all. Any program could read or write *any* memory address, including the OS itself or another program's data.
- **(B)** is also correct: in practice, the only "protection" was that the operator manually removed one program before loading the next — so they never actually overlapped in memory.

Both capture the reality: the safety came from **sequential operation + human oversight**, not from any OS mechanism. OSTEP Chapter 2 describes this era as when "protection between running programs simply did not exist." The lesson: hardware memory protection (the MMU) was a later invention, born from the chaos this arrangement caused.

questions.text: In batch processing, jobs were run one by one by a human operator. What was the main problem for users?

options.A: Users had to wait hours or days to see their program results
options.B: Programs could not be saved and had to be resubmitted every day
options.C: The operator could only run programs written by the same company
options.D: Only programs shorter than one punch card could be submitted

questions.explanation:
**Batch processing** (1950s–60s) automated the sequential execution of jobs, but users still experienced enormous latency.

**Why A is correct:** Jobs were submitted as decks of punch cards, collected into a "batch," and run overnight. A user might wait **24 hours** to discover a single typo. This painful wait drove the invention of time-sharing systems where users interact in real time.

**Why the others are wrong:**
- B: Programs could be arbitrarily long — they were spooled to tape, not limited by a card
- C: Operators ran any job from any user or company
- D: A single punch card could only hold one line; programs filled entire boxes of cards

OSTEP Chapter 2: *"The introduction of time-sharing solved this problem — multiple users could use a machine at once, and each user received a timely response."*

questions.text: Which statements about multiprogramming are correct?

options.A: Each program in memory gets its own dedicated CPU permanently
options.B: Programs run faster because they can share each other's variables
options.C: When one program waits for I/O, another program can use the CPU
options.D: Keeping several programs in memory reduces wasted CPU idle time

questions.explanation:
**Multiprogramming** keeps several programs in memory simultaneously and switches between them when one blocks on I/O.

**Why C and D are correct:**
- **(C)** When a program calls `read()` or waits for a disk, it blocks. Instead of wasting the CPU, the OS runs *another* program. This is **I/O overlap**.
- **(D)** With several programs in memory, the CPU is almost always doing something useful — idle time drops dramatically.

**Why A and B are wrong:**
- A: There is only *one* CPU. No program gets a dedicated CPU — they *share* it by taking turns.
- B: Programs have **isolated address spaces**. They cannot touch each other's variables. That's the whole point of virtual memory.

OSTEP Chapter 2: *"By allowing multiple programs to reside in memory at the same time, the CPU is better utilized."*

questions.text: Linux became popular in the 1990s mainly because it was:

options.A: faster than all other operating systems available at the time
options.B: developed by a large company with strong legal protection
options.C: free, open source, and built on proven UNIX design principles
options.D: the only OS that could run on the new personal computers

questions.explanation:
**Linux** was created by Linus Torvalds in 1991 as a free alternative to expensive UNIX systems. It spread rapidly because of three properties:

1. **Free**: zero licensing cost — any student or company could download it
2. **Open source**: the full source code was visible, auditable, and modifiable
3. **Built on UNIX design**: decades of battle-tested design principles from Bell Labs

**Why the others are wrong:**
- A: Linux was not faster than everything — Solaris, IRIX, and others were competitive
- B: Torvalds was a university student, not a large company with legal teams
- D: DOS and Windows already ran on PCs; Linux was an *alternative* to them, not the only option

OSTEP Chapter 2: *"Linux borrowed heavily from UNIX and made it available for free."* The open-source movement around Linux changed the industry permanently.

questions.text: `counter = 10`. Thread A loads 10. Before A stores, Thread B also loads 10. Both add 1. Both store. What is the final value of `counter`?

options.A: 12 — both threads each added 1 successfully to the value
options.B: 11 — one increment was lost because both threads read the same value
options.C: 10 — the two stores overwrote each other and cancelled out
options.D: 20 — each thread doubled the value before storing its result

questions.explanation:
This is the canonical **race condition** example from OSTEP Chapter 26.

**Trace of the dangerous interleaving:**
```
Thread A: load counter → gets 10
Thread B: load counter → gets 10   (A hasn't stored yet!)
Thread A: add 1 → 11, store 11
Thread B: add 1 → 11, store 11
Final value: 11  (should be 12 — one increment is lost)
```

**Why B (11) is correct:** Both threads read the *same* value of 10, both compute 11, both store 11. One update is silently discarded.

**Why the others are wrong:**
- A (12): Only possible if the operations were truly sequential (no overlap)
- C (10): Would require both stores to cancel out, which doesn't happen this way
- D (20): Both threads doubling is not what the code does

This is why the OS provides **locks and synchronization primitives** — so that read-modify-write sequences are *atomic* (indivisible).

questions.text: Which is an example of a scheduling **policy**, not a mechanism?

options.A: Saving all CPU registers into the process control block
options.B: Switching the CPU from user mode to kernel mode on a trap
options.C: Jumping to the OS interrupt handler when the timer fires
options.D: Running the process that has been waiting the longest first

questions.explanation:
OSTEP Chapter 6 draws a fundamental OS design distinction:

- A **mechanism** answers *"HOW?"* — the low-level procedure to implement something
- A **policy** answers *"WHICH?"* or *"WHEN?"* — the decision about what to do

**Why D is the policy:** "Run the process that has waited the longest" is a *decision rule* — it describes the scheduling strategy (FIFO). It doesn't tell you how to save registers or switch modes.

**Why A, B, C are mechanisms:**
- A: Saving CPU registers to the PCB is the mechanical *how* of a context switch
- B: Switching CPU modes on a trap is a hardware *procedure*
- C: Jumping to an interrupt handler is a hardware *mechanism*

OSTEP: *"The separation of policy and mechanism is important because it allows you to change policies without changing the underlying mechanism."* For example, you can swap FIFO for SJF without changing how context switches work.

questions.text: Which are main OS responsibilities?

options.A: Virtualization, making physical resources appear as many virtual ones
options.B: Compilation, turning source code into executable binary files
options.C: Persistence, keeping data safe even when power is lost
options.D: Defragmentation, reorganizing disk files to improve read speed

questions.explanation:
OSTEP Chapter 2 identifies three main OS responsibilities:

1. **Virtualization** — make physical resources (CPU, memory) appear as many virtual ones
2. **Concurrency** — safely handle multiple simultaneous operations
3. **Persistence** — keep data safe even when power is lost

**Why A and C are correct:**
- A (Virtualization): The OS creates the illusion of private CPU and memory per process — this *is* virtualization
- C (Persistence): File systems, journaling, fsync — all ensure data survives power loss

**Why B and D are wrong:**
- B (Compilation): The *compiler* (gcc, javac) turns source into binaries. The OS just runs them.
- D (Defragmentation): A storage utility, not a core OS responsibility. The OS manages *allocation*, not disk optimization.

questions.text: A database writes records to disk. The computer crashes halfway through. On restart, some records are missing. Which OS responsibility failed?

options.A: Concurrency — two threads wrote to the database at the same time
options.B: Resource Management — the CPU was not shared correctly between tasks
options.C: Persistence — the write was not completed and made safe on disk
options.D: Virtualization — memory addresses were mapped to the wrong location

questions.explanation:
A database crash mid-write is a textbook **Persistence** failure.

**Why C is correct:** The OS's file system is responsible for ensuring writes are **durable** and **consistent**. Modern file systems use *journaling* (recording intended operations before doing them) or *copy-on-write* so that a crash leaves the disk in a consistent state. If records are missing after a crash, the persistence layer failed.

**Why the others are wrong:**
- A (Concurrency): Only one process was writing — no concurrent conflict
- B (Resource Management): CPU scheduling had no role in this failure
- D (Virtualization): The memory mapping was fine — the issue was disk durability

OSTEP Chapters 39–42 cover file systems and **crash consistency** in detail. Key tool: `fsync()` — forces the OS to flush data to durable storage before returning.

questions.text: Program X stores a value at virtual address `0x2000`. Program Y also has a variable at virtual address `0x2000`. Program X changes its value. What happens to Program Y's variable?

options.A: Y still holds its original value — each address maps to a different physical location
options.B: Y also changes — both programs share the same physical memory cell
options.C: An error occurs — two programs cannot use the same virtual address
options.D: Y is set to zero — the OS protects Y by zeroing the shared cell

questions.explanation:
This is the core of **virtual memory**: every process has its own *private* virtual address space.

**Why A is correct:** Virtual address `0x2000` in Program X maps to physical address P1. Virtual address `0x2000` in Program Y maps to a *completely different* physical address P2. The OS (via the **MMU** — Memory Management Unit) maintains a separate **page table** for each process. When X writes to 0x2000, it modifies P1. Y's 0x2000 still points to P2 — completely unaffected.

**Why B, C, D are wrong:**
- B: No shared physical cell — virtual address isolation is absolute
- C: Many programs can use the same virtual address simultaneously — that's the point
- D: The OS doesn't zero anything; it just keeps separate mappings

OSTEP Chapter 15: *"The OS creates the illusion that each program has a large, private address space, when in reality multiple programs are sharing physical memory."*

questions.text: What is the correct term for giving each process a small slice of CPU time, switching quickly between them?

options.A: Space sharing
options.B: Batch processing
options.C: Time sharing
options.D: Multiprogramming

questions.explanation:
The technique of rapidly switching CPU between processes to create the *illusion* of simultaneous execution is called **time sharing**.

**Why C is correct:** The CPU runs Process 1 for 10ms, then Process 2 for 10ms, then Process 3 — so fast (100–1000 switches/second) that humans perceive them running at the same time.

**Why the others are wrong:**
- A (Space sharing): Refers to dividing memory, not CPU time
- B (Batch processing): The sequential, non-interactive old model — opposite of simultaneous
- D (Multiprogramming): A related but broader concept — keeping multiple programs in memory to reduce CPU idle time. Time sharing is the *specific* mechanism of rapid CPU switching for interactivity.

OSTEP Chapter 4: *"By time sharing the CPU, it virtualizes the CPU — each process believes it has its own dedicated CPU, when in reality the OS is switching between them rapidly."*

questions.text: Which statements about kernel mode and user mode are correct?

options.A: In kernel mode, the OS can access any memory or hardware device
options.B: In user mode, programs must use system calls to request OS services
options.C: In kernel mode, the OS executes with the same limits as user mode
options.D: In user mode, programs run faster because fewer checks are performed

questions.explanation:
The CPU hardware supports two privilege levels: **kernel mode** and **user mode**.

**Why A and B are correct:**
- **(A)** In kernel mode, the OS runs with full hardware access — any memory address, any instruction, any hardware register. The hardware imposes no restrictions.
- **(B)** In user mode, programs cannot directly access hardware or perform privileged operations. They *must* go through **system calls** to request OS services.

**Why C and D are wrong:**
- C: Kernel mode has *fewer* restrictions than user mode, not the same limits
- D: User mode is not faster — the restrictions exist for safety, not speed. Kernel mode runs at the same CPU speed.

OSTEP Chapter 6: *"The hardware provides two modes: kernel mode (OS code runs here, full access) and user mode (user programs run here, restricted access). A special trap instruction elevates to kernel mode."*

questions.text: Why does the OS run in kernel mode while user programs run in user mode?

options.A: Kernel mode makes the OS code execute at a faster clock speed
options.B: User mode prevents programs from directly accessing hardware or other programs' memory
options.C: Kernel mode allows the OS to use more RAM than user programs can
options.D: User mode programs cannot create new processes without switching modes

questions.explanation:
Hardware user/kernel mode separation is the foundation of OS security and stability.

**Why B is correct:** If user programs could directly access hardware, any program could:
- Overwrite OS kernel data
- Read another process's memory (passwords, encryption keys)
- Crash the entire system
- Control hardware devices directly

User mode is a *hardware enforcement* of the principle of least privilege. Programs can only do what the OS allows via **system calls**.

**Why the others are wrong:**
- A: Kernel mode doesn't increase clock speed — it's the same hardware
- C: Kernel mode doesn't allow *more RAM* — it's about *instruction privileges*, not memory capacity
- D: Processes can be created via `fork()` in user mode — that's a system call, but it doesn't require "switching modes" as the reason for the restriction

OSTEP Chapter 6: *"Limited direct execution — programs run directly on the hardware, but the OS limits what they can do."*

questions.text: What is the difference between a **program** and a **process**?

options.A: A program is temporary; a process is stored permanently on disk
options.B: A program runs in kernel mode; a process runs only in user mode
options.C: A program uses memory; a process is a file waiting to be loaded
options.D: A program is a file on disk; a process is that program actively running

questions.explanation:
The program/process distinction is fundamental.

**Why D is correct:**
- A **program** is a *static* artifact: an executable file on disk (`.exe`, `.py`, `a.out`). It contains compiled instructions but does nothing by itself.
- A **process** is a *dynamic* instance: the program *loaded into memory*, with a program counter, registers, stack, heap, and state. It is actively executing.

**One program, many processes:** You can run the same program 100 times simultaneously — 1 program file, 100 process instances.

**Why A, B, C are wrong:**
- A: Reversed — the *process* is temporary (exists while running); the *program* persists on disk
- B: Both programs and processes can involve kernel mode transitions
- C: Completely reversed — the program is the file; the process is the running instance

OSTEP Chapter 4: *"A process is simply a running program."*

questions.text: You run the same Python script 4 times at the same time. How many programs and processes are there?

options.A: 1 program and 4 processes
options.B: 4 programs and 4 processes
options.C: 4 programs and 1 process
options.D: 1 program and 1 process

questions.explanation:
Running the same script 4 times demonstrates the program/process distinction clearly.

**Why A is correct:**
- There is **1 program** — one copy of the Python script file on disk
- There are **4 processes** — each `python script.py` invocation creates a new, independent process with its own PID, memory space (stack, heap, registers)

Each of the 4 processes thinks it has the entire machine to itself. They cannot access each other's variables. Each has a unique process ID assigned by the OS.

**Why B is wrong:** The file isn't copied — there's still one script file, just run multiple times.
**Why C is wrong:** One file, multiple instances — not four files, one instance.
**Why D is wrong:** 4 executions = 4 processes.

OSTEP Chapter 4: *"The OS creates the illusion of a nearly endless supply of CPUs by virtualizing the CPU."*

questions.text:
A process has `int counter = 0;` declared at the top of the file, outside any function. Where is this stored in the address space?

```
HIGH ADDRESS
┌──────────────┐
│    Stack     │
│    (free)    │
│    Heap      │
├──────────────┤
│  Static Data │
├──────────────┤
│     Code     │
└──────────────┘
LOW ADDRESS
```

options.A: Stack — it is used inside function calls at runtime
options.B: Heap — it can grow and shrink while the program runs
options.C: Static Data — it is a global variable with a fixed location
options.D: Code — it is part of the compiled binary instructions

questions.explanation:
The **address space** of a process has four main regions:

```
HIGH ADDRESS
┌──────────────┐
│    Stack     │  ← local variables, function call frames (grows down)
│    (free)    │
│    Heap      │  ← dynamic allocation: malloc(), new (grows up)
├──────────────┤
│  Static Data │  ← global variables, static variables (fixed size)
├──────────────┤
│     Code     │  ← compiled instructions (read-only)
└──────────────┘
LOW ADDRESS
```

**Why C is correct:** `int counter = 0` declared *outside any function* is a **global variable**. Global variables have a fixed address that exists for the entire program lifetime — they live in **Static Data**.

**Why the others are wrong:**
- A (Stack): Stack is for *local* variables inside functions — they're destroyed when the function returns
- B (Heap): Heap is for *explicitly allocated* memory (`new`, `malloc`)
- D (Code): Code stores *compiled instructions*, not data

OSTEP Chapter 13: *"The static data segment contains global and static variables."*

questions.text:
Why does P0 change from READY to RUNNING at time 5?

```
Time | P0        | P1
  1  | READY     | RUNNING
  2  | READY     | RUNNING
  3  | READY     | RUNNING   ← P1 sends data over the network
  4  | READY     | BLOCKED
  5  | RUNNING   | BLOCKED
```

options.A: P0 has a higher scheduling priority than P1 in this system
options.B: A timer fired at time 4 and the OS randomly selected P0
options.C: P0 requested more memory and was rewarded with CPU time
options.D: P1 is waiting for network I/O, so the OS runs P0 instead

questions.explanation:
This schedule illustrates **I/O-triggered context switching**.

**Why D is correct:** At time 3, P1 initiates a network send — a slow I/O operation (network operations take milliseconds). The OS immediately moves P1 to **BLOCKED** state at time 4 (waiting for the network). Since P0 is **READY** and the CPU is free, the scheduler runs P0 at time 5.

**Why the others are wrong:**
- A: P0 doesn't have higher scheduling priority — P1 ran first because P0 wasn't RUNNING
- B: A random timer selection would be preemptive scheduling, not what's shown
- C: P0 doesn't request memory to get CPU time

OSTEP Chapter 4: *"When a process initiates an I/O request, it is moved to the BLOCKED state. The scheduler then picks another READY process to run."* This I/O overlap is precisely why multiprogramming improves CPU utilization.

questions.text: Which items are stored in the Process Control Block (PCB)?

options.A: The process ID (PID) and the ID of its parent process
options.B: The complete program binary loaded from disk at startup
options.C: A log of every system call the process has made so far
options.D: Information about which files and devices the process has open

questions.explanation:
The **Process Control Block (PCB)** is the OS's data structure for tracking each process — OSTEP Chapter 4 calls it a "process list" entry.

**Why A and D are correct:**
- **(A)** PID and parent PID are fundamental identifiers. The parent PID enables process trees (needed for `wait()`, signals, job control).
- **(D)** Open file descriptors and device handles are tracked in the PCB. The OS must know what resources each process holds so it can clean up on exit.

**Why B and C are wrong:**
- B: The program binary lives on disk and is mapped into memory — not stored in the PCB
- C: System call logs are not stored per-process in the PCB (they might exist in audit logs, but that's separate)

**Other PCB contents:** saved CPU registers, memory mappings (page table pointer), scheduling priority, accounting info (CPU time used), signal handlers. OSTEP shows the PCB as the central data structure for process management.

options.A: only "hello" — the parent process exits right after fork()
options.B: only "world" — the child process never reaches the output line
options.C: "hello" then "world"
options.D: "world" then "hello"

questions.explanation:
After `fork()`, two independent processes exist and the scheduler can run either first.

**Trace:**
```
Parent: rc = fork()  →  rc = child's PID (> 0)  →  prints "world"
Child:  rc = fork()  →  rc = 0                  →  prints "hello"
```

**Why C and D are correct:** The OS scheduler decides execution order *after* fork. There is **no guarantee** which runs first. Both orderings are valid:
- C: Child runs first → "hello
world"
- D: Parent runs first → "world
hello"

**Why A and B are wrong:**
- A: The parent does *not* exit right after fork — it continues to the `else` branch and prints "world"
- B: The child does *not* skip the output — it reaches `std::cout << "hello"` via the `if (rc == 0)` branch

OSTEP Chapter 5: *"After fork, the scheduler determines which process runs next — the parent or child. We cannot make any assumptions about the ordering."*

questions.text: A child process was just created by fork(). What value does the **child** receive as the return value of fork()?

options.A: 0 — the child always receives zero from fork()
options.B: Its own PID — so it knows its own process identity
options.C: The parent's PID — so it knows who created it
options.D: -1 — the value that signals success in UNIX system calls

questions.explanation:
`fork()` uses a clever trick to tell parent and child apart with a single return value.

| Who | Return value of fork() |
|-----|------------------------|
| **Child** | `0` |
| **Parent** | Child's PID (positive) |
| **Error** | `-1` |

**Why A is correct:** The child always receives `0`. This is a *signal*, not a PID — it simply means "you are the newly created child." The child can call `getpid()` if it needs its own PID.

**Why the others are wrong:**
- B: The child doesn't receive its own PID from fork — it gets 0
- C: The parent's PID is not what fork returns in the child
- D: `-1` means fork *failed*, not succeeded

OSTEP Chapter 5: *"The child gets 0 back from fork(); the parent gets the PID of the newly-created child."* This design makes the common `if (rc == 0)` child-detection pattern possible.

questions.text: What is the main weakness of cooperative scheduling?

options.A: It switches between programs too often, causing too much overhead
options.B: It requires a hardware timer that not all computers support
options.C: A program that never calls a system call can run forever, blocking others
options.D: Programs must be written in a special way to give up the CPU

questions.explanation:
**Cooperative scheduling** relies on processes voluntarily yielding the CPU. This worked on simple systems but has a fatal flaw.

**Why C is correct:** If a process enters an infinite loop and *never* calls a system call or `yield()`, the OS never gets control back. The system **freezes** until reboot. The OS is helpless — it needs the CPU to act, but the CPU is trapped in the loop.

**Why the others are wrong:**
- A: Cooperative OS doesn't kill processes — it can't, because it never gets the CPU back
- B: Timers exist in preemptive scheduling, *not* cooperative scheduling
- D: Loops don't auto-yield on overflow — the code has to explicitly call yield

**The solution:** **Preemptive scheduling** + a hardware timer interrupt. Every N milliseconds (typically 10ms), the timer fires and the hardware transfers control to the OS interrupt handler, regardless of what the process is doing. OSTEP Chapter 6: *"The addition of the timer interrupt gives the OS the ability to run again on a CPU."*

questions.text:
A timer fires while Process A is running. Which sequence is correct?

```
1: CPU switches to kernel mode and jumps to the interrupt handler
2: OS saves A's kernel registers into A's process structure
3: Timer interrupt fires while A is running
4: OS loads B's registers from B's process structure and jumps to B
5: Hardware saves A's user-level registers onto the kernel stack
```
questions.explanation:
The **timer interrupt** context switch sequence has two distinct phases — hardware and software.

**Correct order: 3 → 5 → 1 → 2 → 4**

Step by step:
1. **(3) Timer fires** — hardware detects the interrupt
2. **(5) Hardware saves user-level registers** onto the *kernel stack* — this is **automatic**, done by CPU hardware with no OS involvement
3. **(1) CPU switches to kernel mode and jumps to interrupt handler** — hardware changes privilege level
4. **(2) OS saves kernel registers into PCB** — this is **software**, the OS context switch code explicitly saves registers like SP, PC to the process's PCB
5. **(4) OS loads new process's registers and jumps to it** — the new process runs

**Key insight:** The *first* save (step 5) is done by hardware automatically. The *second* save (step 2) is done by OS software. Both are necessary.

OSTEP Chapter 6 shows this two-phase save as a crucial detail of context switching.

questions.text: When a timer interrupt fires, the hardware saves the current process's registers. Where are they saved?

options.A: Into the process control block (PCB) in main memory
options.B: Into a special register file inside the CPU core
options.C: Into the kernel stack for the current process
options.D: Into a region of the user process's own stack memory

questions.explanation:
When a timer interrupt fires, the CPU automatically saves registers *before* the OS can act.

**Why C is correct:** The hardware saves the interrupted process's registers onto that process's **kernel stack** — a separate per-process stack in kernel memory. This is a hardware operation (the CPU does it automatically, not software). The OS then reads these values and saves them to the PCB.

**Why the others are wrong:**
- A (PCB directly): The hardware saves to kernel stack *first*; OS software later moves them to PCB
- B (special register file): There is no "register file" for saving interrupt context — it uses the kernel stack
- D (user stack): Saving to the user stack would be insecure — user code could modify saved registers

OSTEP Chapter 6: *"When the timer interrupt occurs, the hardware saves the registers of the currently-running process onto the kernel stack."* This is the first of two saves in a full context switch.

questions.text: Which are hidden costs of a context switch?

options.A: The process must be recompiled before it can run again
options.B: Memory used by the old process must be freed immediately
options.C: The CPU pipeline is flushed, wasting partially processed instructions
options.D: The CPU cache holds data from the old process, causing misses for the new one

questions.explanation:
Beyond the explicit register save/restore, context switches have **hidden performance costs**.

**Why C and D are correct:**
- **(C) CPU pipeline flush:** Modern CPUs execute instructions in stages (fetch, decode, execute, writeback). When a context switch occurs mid-pipeline, all partially-processed instructions are discarded — wasted work.
- **(D) Cache invalidation:** The CPU cache is warm for the old process (its data is in L1/L2 cache). After switching, the new process accesses *different* memory. Every cache miss requires fetching from RAM (~100× slower). The system runs slowly until the cache warms up again for the new process.

**Why A and B are wrong:**
- A: Code is never reloaded from disk — it's memory-mapped and stays in RAM
- B: Memory is *not* freed on a context switch — the process still owns its memory

OSTEP Chapter 6: *"Cache pollution is a hidden cost — running a new process evicts the old process's cache contents, slowing the first few instructions of the new process."*

questions.text: Why must only the OS kernel be allowed to control the hardware timer?

options.A: Timer hardware requires kernel-level assembly code to program correctly
options.B: Two programs sharing timer access would cause the timer to lose accuracy
options.C: A program that stops the timer could take over the CPU and never be interrupted
options.D: The timer is the only mechanism the OS has to forcibly regain control of the CPU

questions.explanation:
The hardware timer is the OS's **only mechanism for preemptive control**.

**Why C and D are correct:**
- **(C)** If a user program disables the timer, it can run forever without interruption. No other mechanism can forcibly preempt it — the OS would lose control permanently.
- **(D)** The timer interrupt is literally the *only* hardware signal the OS uses to forcibly reclaim the CPU from a running process. Without it, the OS must wait for the process to voluntarily yield.

**Why A and B are wrong:**
- A: Timer circuits don't require special assembly — the issue is *privilege*, not difficulty
- B: Two programs sharing timer control is a scheduling problem, not a hardware accuracy issue

OSTEP Chapter 6: *"The OS must control the hardware timer to ensure it can run again. If a user program could stop the timer, it would own the CPU forever."* This is why timer control is a privileged operation, available only in kernel mode.

questions.text: Response time is defined as:

options.A: T_completion − T_arrival
options.B: T_firstrun − T_arrival
options.C: T_completion − T_firstrun
options.D: T_arrival − T_firstrun

questions.explanation:
OSTEP Chapter 7 defines two distinct performance metrics:

| Metric | Formula | Measures |
|--------|---------|---------|
| **Turnaround time** | T_completion − T_arrival | Total time from submission to completion |
| **Response time** | T_firstrun − T_arrival | How long until first CPU access |

**Why B is correct:** Response time = T_firstrun − T_arrival captures *interactivity* — how quickly does the system react to a new job?

**Why the others are wrong:**
- A: That's *turnaround time*, not response time
- C: T_completion − T_firstrun measures execution time (how long the job actually ran), not a standard metric
- D: Negative value — would be nonsensical

Response time matters for interactive systems (users notice delays > 100ms). Turnaround matters for batch systems (minimize total job completion time).

questions.text: [Calculation] Three jobs arrive at time 0: A=20s, B=20s, C=20s. FIFO: A→B→C. Average turnaround time?
questions.explanation:
**FIFO with equal-length jobs** — calculate turnaround times:

```
Timeline: |── A (20s) ──|── B (20s) ──|── C (20s) ──|
           t=0          t=20          t=40           t=60
```

| Job | Finish time | Arrival | Turnaround |
|-----|------------|---------|-----------|
| A   | 20         | 0       | 20s       |
| B   | 40         | 0       | 40s       |
| C   | 60         | 0       | 60s       |

**Average = (20 + 40 + 60) / 3 = 120 / 3 = 40s**

With *equal-length* jobs, FIFO is actually optimal — no other ordering would improve the average. The problem arises only when job lengths differ (the "convoy effect").

questions.text: [Calculation] Three jobs arrive at time 0: A=90s, B=5s, C=5s. FIFO: A→B→C. Average turnaround time?
questions.explanation:
**FIFO convoy effect** — one long job blocks all the short ones.

```
Timeline: |──────────── A (90s) ────────────|─B─|─C─|
           t=0                               t=90 t=95 t=100
```

| Job | Finish | Turnaround |
|-----|--------|-----------|
| A   | 90     | 90s       |
| B   | 95     | 95s       |
| C   | 100    | 100s      |

**Average = (90 + 95 + 100) / 3 = 285 / 3 = 95s**

B and C are only 5 seconds of work but must wait 90 seconds! If SJF were used (B→C→A): turnaround would be (5 + 10 + 100)/3 = 38.3s — a huge improvement.

OSTEP Chapter 7: *"The convoy effect occurs when a number of relatively-short potential consumers of a resource get queued behind a heavyweight resource consumer."*

questions.text: [Calculation] Three jobs arrive at time 0: A=8s, B=3s, C=12s. SJF (shortest first). What is the **response time** for job A?

options.A: 3s
options.B: 8s
options.C: 0s

questions.explanation:
**SJF** (Shortest Job First) reorders jobs by length. With A=8s, B=3s, C=12s:

```
SJF order: B (3s) → A (8s) → C (12s)
Timeline:  |─B─|────A────|────────C────────|
            t=0  t=3     t=11              t=23
```

**Response time for job A = T_firstrun − T_arrival = 3 − 0 = 3s**

A first gets CPU at t=3 (after B finishes). It arrived at t=0. So response time = 3s.

**Why the other options are wrong:**
- B (8s): That would be A's response time if it ran first, or B's execution duration
- C (0s): A doesn't start immediately — B goes first (B is shorter)
- D (11s): That's A's *turnaround* time, not response time

OSTEP Chapter 7: Response time = first moment the job gets the CPU, not when it finishes.

questions.text: [Calculation] Round Robin, time slice = 2s, 5 jobs all arriving at time 0. Worst-case response time?

options.A: 2s
options.B: 4s
options.D: 8s

questions.explanation:
**Round Robin** worst-case response time with N jobs and time slice Q:

**Formula: (N − 1) × Q**

With 5 jobs and time slice = 2s:
```
t=0:  Job 1 runs (response time = 0)
t=2:  Job 2 runs (response time = 2)
t=4:  Job 3 runs (response time = 4)
t=6:  Job 4 runs (response time = 6)
t=8:  Job 5 runs (response time = 8)
```

**Worst-case response time = (5 − 1) × 2 = 8s** (for the last job in the rotation)

**Why:** In the worst case, a job arrives just *after* being skipped and must wait for all N−1 other jobs to each get one time slice before it gets its first turn.

OSTEP Chapter 7: *"RR is fair — every job gets a turn quickly — making it excellent for response time but poor for turnaround time."*

questions.text: A student says: "SJF is the best scheduler so we should always use it." What is wrong with this?

options.A: SJF requires too much memory to keep all jobs sorted by length
options.B: SJF can only handle a maximum of 10 jobs at once
options.C: The OS has no way of knowing in advance how long each job will run
options.D: SJF only works correctly on computers with multiple CPU cores

questions.explanation:
**SJF is theoretically optimal for turnaround time but practically impossible.**

**Why C is correct:** The OS cannot know how long a job will run before it starts. Job length is only known *after* the job completes. You can estimate it (using exponential averaging of past CPU bursts), but exact prediction is impossible. A process might exit early due to user input, hit an unexpected branch, or encounter a network timeout.

**Why the others are wrong:**
- A: SJF uses no more memory than FIFO — job length is just a number, not extra data structures
- B: SJF works even with different arrival times (STCF — Shortest Time-to-Completion First — handles this)
- D: SJF needs no special hardware

OSTEP Chapter 7: *"SJF is the provably optimal algorithm if we know job lengths in advance — but we generally don't, so it cannot be used directly."* This is why MLFQ was invented: to approximate SJF without requiring foreknowledge.

questions.text: Which statements about Round Robin are correct?

options.A: Round Robin gives each job a fast first turn, improving response time
options.B: Round Robin gives very low average turnaround time for most workloads
options.C: Round Robin shares the CPU fairly by giving each job a fixed time slice
options.D: Round Robin must know each job's total run time before it can start

questions.explanation:
**Round Robin** trades turnaround time for response time.

**Why A and C are correct:**
- **(A)** Every job gets its first turn within (N−1) × timeslice. This guarantees *fast response time* — great for interactive systems.
- **(C)** RR operates by giving each job a fixed time slice (quantum), then moving to the next. This is its defining mechanism.

**Why B and D are wrong:**
- B: RR has *terrible* average turnaround time. It stretches all jobs out equally instead of finishing short ones first. OSTEP Chapter 7: *"RR is one of the worst policies for turnaround time."*
- D: RR needs *no* foreknowledge — it just rotates regardless of job length. This is an advantage over SJF.

OSTEP Chapter 7: *"RR trades turnaround time for response time — it optimizes for interactivity at the expense of completion efficiency."*

questions.text: A job uses 8ms of CPU, then does I/O. Allotment per level = 20ms. After I/O, what is its priority?

options.A: Reduced — the job used some CPU time, even under the allotment
options.B: Moved to lowest — all jobs that do I/O are moved to the bottom
options.C: Reset to top — finishing I/O always triggers a full priority reset
options.D: Unchanged — the job has not used up its full 20ms allotment yet

questions.explanation:
**MLFQ Rule 4** (OSTEP Chapter 8): A job's allotment tracks CPU time used at its *current queue level*, including across I/O operations.

**Why D is correct:** The job used 8ms of its 20ms allotment. It has **12ms remaining**. After returning from I/O, it re-enters at the **same priority level** (same queue) with its remaining allotment intact. The job is NOT demoted because it hasn't exhausted its allotment.

**Why the others are wrong:**
- A: Not demoted — partial allotment use doesn't cause demotion
- B: I/O doesn't automatically drop a job to the lowest queue
- C: I/O completion does NOT reset the allotment — that loophole was explicitly closed to prevent "gaming" the scheduler

**The key insight:** I/O-intensive jobs (like text editors, web browsers) naturally stay at high priority — they voluntarily give up the CPU frequently, never exhausting their allotment. MLFQ rewards this behavior.

questions.text: A CPU-bound job enters MLFQ. Q2 (high) → Q1 → Q0 (low). Allotment = 15ms. No I/O. After 45ms, which queue?

options.A: Q0 — demoted twice: Q2→Q1, then Q1→Q0
options.B: Q1 — demoted once after using Q2's allotment
options.C: Q1 — reaching Q0 requires more than 30ms of CPU use
options.D: Q2 — using the CPU alone does not change a job's priority

questions.explanation:
**CPU-bound job demotion trace** with allotment = 15ms per level:

```
t=0:   Enters Q2 (highest priority)
t=15:  Exhausted Q2 allotment → DEMOTED to Q1
t=30:  Exhausted Q1 allotment → DEMOTED to Q0
t=45:  Now at Q0 (lowest priority)
```

**Why A is correct:** After 3 × 15ms = 45ms with no I/O, the job has been demoted twice. It sits at Q0.

This is MLFQ "learning" that the job is CPU-bound. It progressively reduces its priority, saving the high-priority queues for interactive/short jobs. A pure CPU job at Q0 gets the least priority but still runs — it's not killed, just deprioritized.

OSTEP Chapter 8 Rule 4: *"Once a job uses up its time allotment at a given level, its priority is reduced."*

questions.text: If MLFQ never uses priority boost, what happens to a CPU-bound job at Q0?

options.A: It gets moved to a higher queue after it has waited long enough
options.B: It keeps running but slower, since Q0 receives less CPU power
options.C: It may never run again if interactive jobs keep arriving at Q2
options.D: It receives CPU time each time a new job arrives to the system

questions.explanation:
**Starvation** — the problem MLFQ priority boost was designed to solve.

**Why C is correct:** Without a periodic priority boost:
1. Interactive jobs keep arriving at Q2 (highest priority)
2. The scheduler always picks Q2 jobs first
3. The CPU-bound job at Q0 *never* runs — it is **starved**

This can last indefinitely. A batch job submitted Friday afternoon might not run until the interactive workload decreases — or never.

**Why the others are wrong:**
- A: Jobs that stay high-priority don't get moved — only Q0 jobs are at risk
- B: Interactive jobs *stay* high unless they start using CPU heavily
- D: The job does NOT receive CPU on new arrivals — new jobs go to Q2, making the situation worse

OSTEP Chapter 8: *"Without any other mechanism, a process that is demoted to the lowest queue may never run again — this is called starvation. Priority boost solves this."*

questions.text: Two jobs are both in Q1. Neither has a higher priority than the other. How does MLFQ decide which one runs?

options.A: The one that arrived in the system first gets the CPU
options.B: The one that has used less total CPU time so far gets the CPU
options.C: They take turns using the CPU with a fixed time slice
options.D: The one with the lower process ID number gets the CPU

questions.explanation:
**MLFQ Rule 2** (OSTEP Chapter 8): *If two jobs are at the same priority, run them round-robin.*

**Why C is correct:** Within a queue, all jobs are equal. The scheduler uses **Round Robin** among same-level jobs — each gets a time slice, then yields to the next.

**Why the others are wrong:**
- A: FIFO by arrival is NOT the rule — RR is used within a queue
- B: CPU usage history doesn't break ties within a queue
- D: Process ID does not determine scheduling order

This rule ensures **fairness within a priority level**. Two users both running CPU-heavy jobs at Q0 will each get half the CPU time. Neither can monopolize based on having a lower PID or arriving earlier.

questions.text: Which statements about MLFQ are correct?

options.A: Jobs that do frequent I/O tend to stay at higher priority queues
options.B: All jobs always stay in the same queue for their entire time in the system
options.C: A periodic priority boost prevents long-running jobs from being starved
options.D: New jobs always enter at the lowest priority queue to wait their turn

questions.explanation:
**MLFQ property review:**

**Why A and C are correct:**
- **(A)** Jobs that do frequent I/O consistently use only a small fraction of their allotment before blocking. They never exhaust it → never get demoted → **stay at high priority**. MLFQ naturally identifies and rewards I/O-intensive interactive jobs.
- **(C)** The periodic priority boost (Rule 5) moves *all* jobs back to the highest queue after period S. This **prevents starvation** and also gives CPU-bound jobs that have changed behavior (e.g., became interactive) a chance to regain appropriate priority.

**Why B and D are wrong:**
- B: Jobs move constantly — CPU jobs sink, boosts raise everyone
- D: New jobs enter at the **highest** queue (Rule 3), not the lowest. MLFQ assumes new jobs are short until proven otherwise.

questions.text: A job at Q2 uses 9ms of CPU, then does I/O. Allotment per level = 15ms. When it returns from I/O, how much allotment remains at Q2?

options.A: 6ms — the OS tracks total CPU used, even across I/O
options.B: 15ms — the allotment fully resets after every I/O call
options.C: 9ms — previous CPU usage is forgotten when I/O starts
options.D: 0ms — any I/O call uses up all remaining allotment

questions.explanation:
**MLFQ allotment tracking across I/O** (anti-gaming rule):

```
Job at Q2, allotment = 15ms
Used: 9ms of CPU
Remaining: 15 - 9 = 6ms
```

**Why A is correct:** The OS tracks *cumulative* CPU usage per allotment period. After returning from I/O, the job has only **6ms remaining** at Q2. If it uses those 6ms without blocking again, it exhausts the allotment and is demoted to Q1.

**This rule was added specifically to prevent gaming.** Without it, a smart program could issue a trivial I/O call (like `read` of 1 byte) just before its allotment expires, resetting its counter and staying at high priority forever — unfairly hogging the top queue.

OSTEP Chapter 8: *"The scheduler must be designed so programs cannot game it to achieve better service than they deserve."*

questions.text: A new job arrives in MLFQ. It turns out to be very long and CPU-bound. What will MLFQ do with it over time?

options.A: Keep it at the top queue so other jobs can plan around it
options.B: Gradually demote it to lower queues as it uses up each level's allotment
options.C: Move it immediately to the bottom queue since long jobs are not allowed at top
options.D: Terminate it after a fixed time since long jobs are not allowed in MLFQ

questions.explanation:
**MLFQ Rule 3** (OSTEP Chapter 8): *When a job enters the system, it is placed at the highest priority queue.*

**Why B is correct:** MLFQ doesn't know if a new job will be short or long. It *optimistically* places it at Q2 (highest), giving it the benefit of the doubt. If it turns out to be short/interactive, it stays high — good response time. If it's actually long and CPU-bound, it will gradually exhaust allotments and be demoted to Q1, then Q0.

**Why the others are wrong:**
- A: New jobs are not more *urgent*, they're just *unknown* — MLFQ assumes short until proven otherwise
- B/D: Not about urgency or user preference — it's about optimistic assumption

OSTEP Chapter 8: *"MLFQ approximates SJF: by placing new jobs at the top and observing their behavior, it learns over time which are short (interactive) and which are long (CPU-bound)."*

questions.text: Job X runs 1ms then does I/O, repeatedly. Job Y runs non-stop for a long time. After many rounds, where is each job in MLFQ?

options.A: Both at Q0 — all jobs eventually sink to the bottom queue
options.B: X at Q2, Y at Q0 — X never uses its full allotment, Y gets demoted
options.C: Both at Q2 — priority boost keeps resetting everyone to the top
options.D: X at Q0, Y at Q2 — I/O jobs are penalized and CPU jobs are rewarded

questions.explanation:
**Steady-state behavior** of X (I/O-intensive) vs Y (CPU-bound):

**Job X:** Runs 1ms → I/O → repeat. Never uses its full allotment at Q2. Per MLFQ rules, it is **never demoted**. X stays at Q2 indefinitely, getting immediate CPU access each time it returns from I/O.

**Job Y:** Runs continuously. Uses its full allotment at each level. Q2→Q1→Q0. Y sinks to the bottom queue.

**Answer B (X at Q2, Y at Q0)** describes the primary steady-state.
**Answer A (both at Q0)** could occur after many priority boosts where even X is boosted but then... actually X stays high. B is the better answer.

OSTEP Chapter 8: *"MLFQ approximates SJF without requiring foreknowledge — interactive jobs naturally float to the top; CPU-bound jobs sink to the bottom."*

questions.text: MLFQ tries to achieve two goals at once. Which are they?

options.A: Good response time for interactive jobs that do frequent I/O
options.B: Give all jobs exactly equal CPU time over any time period
options.C: Maximize the total number of processes that can run at once
options.D: Good turnaround time by running shorter jobs before longer ones

questions.explanation:
**MLFQ was designed to solve two competing goals simultaneously** (OSTEP Chapter 8):

**Goal 1 — Good turnaround time (D):** By demoting CPU-bound jobs and keeping short jobs at high priority, MLFQ approximates SJF without knowing job lengths in advance. Short jobs finish quickly at high priority.

**Goal 2 — Good response time for interactive jobs (A):** I/O-heavy interactive jobs stay at high priority queues. They get CPU immediately after each I/O, giving users sub-millisecond response times.

**Why B and C are wrong:**
- B: Equal CPU time is *Round Robin's* goal — MLFQ intentionally gives *unequal* time based on behavior
- C: Maximizing simultaneous processes is a memory management goal, not a scheduler goal

OSTEP Chapter 8: *"The fundamental problem MLFQ tries to solve: how do we design a scheduler that behaves like SJF for short interactive jobs, while still being fair to long-running CPU-bound jobs?"*
